info.name: Genesis
info.desc: The First Book of Moses called Genesis

1: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
The Account of Creation
1:1 Or In the beginning when God created the heavens and the earth, . . . Or When God began to create the heavens and the earth, . . .
2: The earth was formless and empty, and darkness covered the deep waters. And the Spirit of God was hovering over the surface of the waters.
The Account of Creation
3: Then God said, “Let there be light,” and there was light.
The Account of Creation
4: And God saw that the light was good. Then he separated the light from the darkness.
The Account of Creation
5:
God called the light “day” and the darkness “night.”
And evening passed and morning came, marking the first day.
The Account of Creation
6: Then God said, “Let there be a space between the waters, to separate the waters of the heavens from the waters of the earth.”
The Account of Creation
7: And that is what happened. God made this space to separate the waters of the earth from the waters of the heavens.
The Account of Creation
8:
God called the space “sky.”
And evening passed and morning came, marking the second day.
The Account of Creation
9: Then God said, “Let the waters beneath the sky flow together into one place, so dry ground may appear.” And that is what happened.
The Account of Creation
10: God called the dry ground “land” and the waters “seas.” And God saw that it was good.
The Account of Creation
11: Then God said, “Let the land sprout with vegetation—every sort of seed-bearing plant, and trees that grow seed-bearing fruit. These seeds will then produce the kinds of plants and trees from which they came.” And that is what happened.
The Account of Creation
12: The land produced vegetation—all sorts of seed-bearing plants, and trees with seed-bearing fruit. Their seeds produced plants and trees of the same kind. And God saw that it was good.
The Account of Creation
13: And evening passed and morning came, marking the third day.
The Account of Creation
14: Then God said, “Let lights appear in the sky to separate the day from the night. Let them be signs to mark the seasons, days, and years.
The Account of Creation
15: Let these lights in the sky shine down on the earth.” And that is what happened.
The Account of Creation
16: God made two great lights—the larger one to govern the day, and the smaller one to govern the night. He also made the stars.
The Account of Creation
17: God set these lights in the sky to light the earth,
The Account of Creation
18: to govern the day and night, and to separate the light from the darkness. And God saw that it was good.
The Account of Creation
19: And evening passed and morning came, marking the fourth day.
The Account of Creation
20: Then God said, “Let the waters swarm with fish and other life. Let the skies be filled with birds of every kind.”
The Account of Creation
21: So God created great sea creatures and every living thing that scurries and swarms in the water, and every sort of bird—each producing offspring of the same kind. And God saw that it was good.
The Account of Creation
22: Then God blessed them, saying, “Be fruitful and multiply. Let the fish fill the seas, and let the birds multiply on the earth.”
The Account of Creation
23: And evening passed and morning came, marking the fifth day.
The Account of Creation
24: Then God said, “Let the earth produce every sort of animal, each producing offspring of the same kind—livestock, small animals that scurry along the ground, and wild animals.” And that is what happened.
The Account of Creation
25: God made all sorts of wild animals, livestock, and small animals, each able to produce offspring of the same kind. And God saw that it was good.
The Account of Creation
26:
Then God said, “Let us make human beings
 in our image, to be like us. They will reign over the fish in the sea, the birds in the sky, the livestock, all the wild animals on the earth,
 and the small animals that scurry along the ground.”
The Account of Creation
1:26b As in Syriac version; Hebrew reads all the earth.; 1:26a Or man; Hebrew reads adam.
27:
So God created human beings
 in his own image.
In the image of God he created them;
male and female he created them.
The Account of Creation
; 1:27 Or the man; Hebrew reads ha-adam.
28: Then God blessed them and said, “Be fruitful and multiply. Fill the earth and govern it. Reign over the fish in the sea, the birds in the sky, and all the animals that scurry along the ground.”
The Account of Creation
29: Then God said, “Look! I have given you every seed-bearing plant throughout the earth and all the fruit trees for your food.
The Account of Creation
30: And I have given every green plant as food for all the wild animals, the birds in the sky, and the small animals that scurry along the ground—everything that has life.” And that is what happened.
31:
Then God looked over all he had made, and he saw that it was very good!
And evening passed and morning came, marking the sixth day.
The Account of Creation

1: So the creation of the heavens and the earth and everything in them was completed.
2:
On the seventh day God had finished his work of creation, so he rested
 from all his work.
2:2 Or ceased; also in 2:3.
3: And God blessed the seventh day and declared it holy, because it was the day when he rested from all his work of creation.
4:
This is the account of the creation of the heavens and the earth.
When the 
Lord
 God made the earth and the heavens,
The Man and Woman in Eden
5:
neither wild plants nor grains were growing on the earth. For the 
Lord
 God had not yet sent rain to water the earth, and there were no people to cultivate the soil.
The Man and Woman in Eden
6:
Instead, springs
 came up from the ground and watered all the land.
The Man and Woman in Eden
2:6 Or mist.
7:
Then the 
Lord
 God formed the man from the dust of the ground. He breathed the breath of life into the man’s nostrils, and the man became a living person.
The Man and Woman in Eden
8:
Then the 
Lord
 God planted a garden in Eden in the east, and there he placed the man he had made.
The Man and Woman in Eden
9:
The 
Lord
 God made all sorts of trees grow up from the ground—trees that were beautiful and that produced delicious fruit. In the middle of the garden he placed the tree of life and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
The Man and Woman in Eden
10: A river flowed from the land of Eden, watering the garden and then dividing into four branches.
The Man and Woman in Eden
11: The first branch, called the Pishon, flowed around the entire land of Havilah, where gold is found.
The Man and Woman in Eden
12: The gold of that land is exceptionally pure; aromatic resin and onyx stone are also found there.
The Man and Woman in Eden
13: The second branch, called the Gihon, flowed around the entire land of Cush.
The Man and Woman in Eden
14: The third branch, called the Tigris, flowed east of the land of Asshur. The fourth branch is called the Euphrates.
The Man and Woman in Eden
15:
The 
Lord
 God placed the man in the Garden of Eden to tend and watch over it.
The Man and Woman in Eden
16:
But the 
Lord
 God warned him, “You may freely eat the fruit of every tree in the garden—
The Man and Woman in Eden
17: except the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. If you eat its fruit, you are sure to die.”
The Man and Woman in Eden
18:
Then the 
Lord
 God said, “It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make a helper who is just right for him.”
The Man and Woman in Eden
19:
So the 
Lord
 God formed from the ground all the wild animals and all the birds of the sky. He brought them to the man
 to see what he would call them, and the man chose a name for each one.
The Man and Woman in Eden
; 2:19 Or Adam, and so throughout the chapter.
20: He gave names to all the livestock, all the birds of the sky, and all the wild animals. But still there was no helper just right for him.
The Man and Woman in Eden
21:
So the 
Lord
 God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep. While the man slept, the 
Lord
 God took out one of the man’s ribs
 and closed up the opening.
The Man and Woman in Eden
; 2:21 Or took a part of the man’s side.
22:
Then the 
Lord
 God made a woman from the rib, and he brought her to the man.
The Man and Woman in Eden
23:
“At last!” the man exclaimed.
“This one is bone from my bone,
and flesh from my flesh!
She will be called ‘woman,’
because she was taken from ‘man.’”
The Man and Woman in Eden
24: This explains why a man leaves his father and mother and is joined to his wife, and the two are united into one.
The Man and Woman in Eden
25: Now the man and his wife were both naked, but they felt no shame.
The Man and Woman in Eden

1:
The serpent was the shrewdest of all the wild animals the 
Lord
 God had made. One day he asked the woman, “Did God really say you must not eat the fruit from any of the trees in the garden?”
The Man and Woman Sin
2: “Of course we may eat fruit from the trees in the garden,” the woman replied.
The Man and Woman Sin
3: “It’s only the fruit from the tree in the middle of the garden that we are not allowed to eat. God said, ‘You must not eat it or even touch it; if you do, you will die.’”
The Man and Woman Sin
4: “You won’t die!” the serpent replied to the woman.
The Man and Woman Sin
5: “God knows that your eyes will be opened as soon as you eat it, and you will be like God, knowing both good and evil.”
The Man and Woman Sin
6: The woman was convinced. She saw that the tree was beautiful and its fruit looked delicious, and she wanted the wisdom it would give her. So she took some of the fruit and ate it. Then she gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it, too.
The Man and Woman Sin
7: At that moment their eyes were opened, and they suddenly felt shame at their nakedness. So they sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves.
The Man and Woman Sin
8:
When the cool evening breezes were blowing, the man
 and his wife heard the 
Lord
 God walking about in the garden. So they hid from the 
Lord
 God among the trees.
The Man and Woman Sin
; 3:8 Or Adam, and so throughout the chapter.
9:
Then the 
Lord
 God called to the man, “Where are you?”
The Man and Woman Sin
10: He replied, “I heard you walking in the garden, so I hid. I was afraid because I was naked.”
The Man and Woman Sin
11:
“Who told you that you were naked?” the 
Lord
 God asked. “Have you eaten from the tree whose fruit I commanded you not to eat?”
The Man and Woman Sin
12: The man replied, “It was the woman you gave me who gave me the fruit, and I ate it.”
The Man and Woman Sin
13:
Then the 
Lord
 God asked the woman, “What have you done?”
“The serpent deceived me,” she replied. “That’s why I ate it.”
The Man and Woman Sin
14:
Then the 
Lord
 God said to the serpent,
“Because you have done this, you are cursed
more than all animals, domestic and wild.
You will crawl on your belly,
groveling in the dust as long as you live.
The Man and Woman Sin
15:
And I will cause hostility between you and the woman,
and between your offspring and her offspring.
He will strike
 your head,
and you will strike his heel.”
The Man and Woman Sin
; 3:15 Or bruise; also in 3:15b.
16:
Then he said to the woman,
“I will sharpen the pain of your pregnancy,
and in pain you will give birth.
And you will desire to control your husband,
but he will rule over you.
”
The Man and Woman Sin
he will rule over you./; 3:16 Or And though you will have desire for your husband,
17:
And to the man he said,
“Since you listened to your wife and ate from the tree
whose fruit I commanded you not to eat,
the ground is cursed because of you.
All your life you will struggle to scratch a living from it.
The Man and Woman Sin
18:
It will grow thorns and thistles for you,
though you will eat of its grains.
The Man and Woman Sin
19:
By the sweat of your brow
will you have food to eat
until you return to the ground
from which you were made.
For you were made from dust,
and to dust you will return.”
Paradise Lost: God’s Judgment
20: Then the man—Adam—named his wife Eve, because she would be the mother of all who live.
Paradise Lost: God’s Judgment
3:20 Eve sounds like a Hebrew term that means “to give life.”
21:
And the 
Lord
 God made clothing from animal skins for Adam and his wife.
Paradise Lost: God’s Judgment
22:
Then the 
Lord
 God said, “Look, the human beings
 have become like us, knowing both good and evil. What if they reach out, take fruit from the tree of life, and eat it? Then they will live forever!”
Paradise Lost: God’s Judgment
; 3:22 Or the man; Hebrew reads ha-adam.
23:
So the 
Lord
 God banished them from the Garden of Eden, and he sent Adam out to cultivate the ground from which he had been made.
Paradise Lost: God’s Judgment
24:
After sending them out, the 
Lord
 God stationed mighty cherubim to the east of the Garden of Eden. And he placed a flaming sword that flashed back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life.
Paradise Lost: God’s Judgment

1:
Now Adam
 had sexual relations with his wife, Eve, and she became pregnant. When she gave birth to Cain, she said, “With the 
Lord
’s help, I have produced
 a man!”
Cain and Abel
; 4:1a Or the man; also in 4:25.; 4:1b Or I have acquired. Cain sounds like a Hebrew term that can mean “produce” or “acquire.”
2:
Later she gave birth to his brother and named him Abel.
When they grew up, Abel became a shepherd, while Cain cultivated the ground.
Cain and Abel
3:
When it was time for the harvest, Cain presented some of his crops as a gift to the 
Lord
.
Cain and Abel
4:
Abel also brought a gift—the best portions of the firstborn lambs from his flock. The 
Lord
 accepted Abel and his gift,
Cain and Abel
5: but he did not accept Cain and his gift. This made Cain very angry, and he looked dejected.
Cain and Abel
6:
“Why are you so angry?” the 
Lord
 asked Cain. “Why do you look so dejected?
Cain and Abel
7: You will be accepted if you do what is right. But if you refuse to do what is right, then watch out! Sin is crouching at the door, eager to control you. But you must subdue it and be its master.”
Cain and Abel
8:
One day Cain suggested to his brother, “Let’s go out into the fields.”
 And while they were in the field, Cain attacked his brother, Abel, and killed him.
Cain and Abel
4:8 As in Samaritan Pentateuch, Greek and Syriac versions, and Latin Vulgate; Masoretic Text lacks “Let’s go out into the fields.”
9:
Afterward the 
Lord
 asked Cain, “Where is your brother? Where is Abel?”
“I don’t know,” Cain responded. “Am I my brother’s guardian?”
Cain and Abel
10:
But the 
Lord
 said, “What have you done? Listen! Your brother’s blood cries out to me from the ground!
Cain and Abel
11: Now you are cursed and banished from the ground, which has swallowed your brother’s blood.
Cain and Abel
12: No longer will the ground yield good crops for you, no matter how hard you work! From now on you will be a homeless wanderer on the earth.”
Cain and Abel
13:
Cain replied to the 
Lord
, “My punishment
 is too great for me to bear!
Cain and Abel
; 4:13 Or My sin.
14: You have banished me from the land and from your presence; you have made me a homeless wanderer. Anyone who finds me will kill me!”
Cain and Abel
15:
The 
Lord
 replied, “No, for I will give a sevenfold punishment to anyone who kills you.” Then the 
Lord
 put a mark on Cain to warn anyone who might try to kill him.
Cain and Abel
16:
So Cain left the 
Lord
’s presence and settled in the land of Nod,
 east of Eden.
The Descendants of Cain
; 4:16 Nod means “wandering.”
17: Cain had sexual relations with his wife, and she became pregnant and gave birth to Enoch. Then Cain founded a city, which he named Enoch, after his son.
The Descendants of Cain
18:
Enoch had a son named Irad. Irad became the father of
 Mehujael. Mehujael became the father of Methushael. Methushael became the father of Lamech.
The Descendants of Cain
4:18 Or the ancestor of, and so throughout the verse.
19: Lamech married two women. The first was named Adah, and the second was Zillah.
The Descendants of Cain
20: Adah gave birth to Jabal, who was the first of those who raise livestock and live in tents.
The Descendants of Cain
21: His brother’s name was Jubal, the first of all who play the harp and flute.
The Descendants of Cain
22: Lamech’s other wife, Zillah, gave birth to a son named Tubal-cain. He became an expert in forging tools of bronze and iron. Tubal-cain had a sister named Naamah.
The Descendants of Cain
23:
One day Lamech said to his wives,
“Adah and Zillah, hear my voice;
listen to me, you wives of Lamech.
I have killed a man who attacked me,
a young man who wounded me.
The Descendants of Cain
24:
If someone who kills Cain is punished seven times,
then the one who kills me will be punished seventy-seven times!”
The Birth of Seth
25:
Adam had sexual relations with his wife again, and she gave birth to another son. She named him Seth,
 for she said, “God has granted me another son in place of Abel, whom Cain killed.”
The Birth of Seth
4:25 Seth probably means “granted”; the name may also mean “appointed.”
26:
When Seth grew up, he had a son and named him Enosh. At that time people first began to worship the 
Lord
 by name.
The Birth of Seth

1:
This is the written account of the descendants of Adam. When God created human beings,
 he made them to be like himself.
The Descendants of Adam
5:1 Or man; Hebrew reads adam; similarly in 5:2.
2: He created them male and female, and he blessed them and called them “human.”
The Descendants of Adam
3: When Adam was 130 years old, he became the father of a son who was just like him—in his very image. He named his son Seth.
The Descendants of Adam
4: After the birth of Seth, Adam lived another 800 years, and he had other sons and daughters.
The Descendants of Adam

When Seth was 105 years old, he became the father of
 Enosh.

 Enosh, Seth lived another 807 years, and he had other sons and daughters.

Lamech named his son Noah, for he said, “May he bring us relief

Lord

; 5:29 Noah sounds like a Hebrew term that can mean “relief” or “comfort.”

1: Joseph threw himself on his father and wept over him and kissed him.
2:
Then Joseph told the physicians who served him to embalm his father’s body; so Jacob

50:2 Hebrew Israel. See note on 35:21.
3: The embalming process took the usual forty days. And the Egyptians mourned his death for seventy days.
4: When the period of mourning was over, Joseph approached Pharaoh’s advisers and said, “Please do me this favor and speak to Pharaoh on my behalf.
5: Tell him that my father made me swear an oath. He said to me, ‘Listen, I am about to die. Take my body back to the land of Canaan, and bury me in the tomb I prepared for myself.’ So please allow me to go and bury my father. After his burial, I will return without delay.”
6: Pharaoh agreed to Joseph’s request. “Go and bury your father, as he made you promise,” he said.
7: So Joseph went up to bury his father. He was accompanied by all of Pharaoh’s officials, all the senior members of Pharaoh’s household, and all the senior officers of Egypt.
8: Joseph also took his entire household and his brothers and their households. But they left their little children and flocks and herds in the land of Goshen.
9: A great number of chariots and charioteers accompanied Joseph.
10: When they arrived at the threshing floor of Atad, near the Jordan River, they held a very great and solemn memorial service, with a seven-day period of mourning for Joseph’s father.
11:
The local residents, the Canaanites, watched them mourning at the threshing floor of Atad. Then they renamed that place (which is near the Jordan) Abel-mizraim,
 for they said, “This is a place of deep mourning for these Egyptians.”
50:11 Abel-mizraim means “mourning of the Egyptians.”
12: So Jacob’s sons did as he had commanded them.
13: They carried his body to the land of Canaan and buried him in the cave in the field of Machpelah, near Mamre. This is the cave that Abraham had bought as a permanent burial site from Ephron the Hittite.
